question: Is Faye cautious?
completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Faye can be described as cautious. The knowledge base provides several conditional statements linking properties like active, inventive, open‑minded, and judicious, as well as a disjunction that directly involves caution. Our goal is to chain these rules to reach a conclusion about Faye’s cautiousness.

The first premise gives us a direct implication: if something is active, it must be inventive. This is essential because we already know Faye is active, so this rule will let us infer her inventiveness, a stepping stone toward later properties.

<text>The rule states that any active entity is inventive; thus, because Faye is active, she must be inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(faye) :- active(faye)</logic>
</step>

The second premise connects open‑mindedness to judiciousness. Once we can establish that Faye is open‑minded, this rule will allow us to move from that state to the judicious property, which is needed for the final disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any open‑minded entity is judicious, linking open‑mindedness to the judicious property.</text>
<logic>judicious(faye) :- open_minded(faye)</logic>
</step>

The third premise creates a link from inventiveness back to open‑mindedness. By having a rule that goes in the opposite direction of the first, we can create a chain: active → inventive → open‑minded, which will be crucial for deriving judiciousness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule claims that any inventive entity is open‑minded, creating a bridge from inventiveness to open‑mindedness.</text>
<logic>open_minded(faye) :- inventive(faye)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise is a disjunction stating that either Faye is not judicious or she is cautious. This logical choice will become decisive once we know whether Faye is judicious; if she is, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for her to be cautious.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction tells us that either Faye is not judicious or she is cautious.</text>
<logic>~judicious(faye) v cautious(faye)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise records that Riley is athletic. Although this fact is not directly used in the derivation of Faye’s cautiousness, it satisfies the requirement to mention all given facts in the context.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Riley possesses the athletic property.</text>
<logic>athletic(riley)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise records that Faye is active. This fact, together with PREMISE_1, initiates the chain that will eventually lead to her being cautious.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Faye possesses the active property.</text>
<logic>active(faye)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens to the fact that Faye is active (PREMISE_6) and the rule that active implies inventive (PREMISE_1), we can confidently assert that Faye is inventive. This step converts a general rule into a specific property about Faye.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From Faye being active and the rule that active implies inventive, we infer that Faye is inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(faye)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Faye’s inventiveness, we use the rule that inventive leads to open‑mindedness (PREMISE_3). By modus ponens again, we deduce that Faye is open‑minded, moving us further along the implication chain.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Using Faye’s newly established inventiveness and the rule that inventive implies open‑minded, we conclude that Faye is open‑minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(faye)</logic>
</step>

With Faye confirmed as open‑minded, we invoke the rule linking open‑mindedness to judiciousness (PREMISE_2). Modus ponens yields that Faye is judicious, giving us the concrete truth value needed for the disjunction.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Given Faye is open‑minded and the rule that open‑minded implies judicious, we derive that Faye is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(faye)</logic>
</step>

We now have the statement that Faye is judicious, which makes the left side of the disjunction "not judicious" false. To keep the disjunction true, the right side must be true, so we conclude that Faye is cautious. This uses disjunctive syllogism to flip the remaining option into a certainty.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Combining the fact that Faye is judicious with the disjunction "not judicious or cautious", we resolve the disjunction to obtain that Faye is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(faye)</logic>

Therefore Faye is cautious, so the conclusion is True.

context: The sunrise is calm. Whenever the sunrise is calm, it is also cloudy. Whenever the sunrise is cloudy, it is sunny. Whenever the sunrise is sunny, it becomes rainy. Either the sunrise is not rainy, or it is storm‑resistant.
question: Is the sunrise storm‑resistant?

We are asked to decide whether the sunrise possesses the property of being storm‑resistant. To answer, we must chain together the given facts and conditional rules until we can either prove or refute storm‑resistance.

This conditional tells us that sunny mornings always bring rain. It will become useful later once we establish that the sunrise is sunny, allowing us to derive raininess.

<step n="2" type="premise">

<logic>cloudy(sunrise) :- calm(sunrise)</logic>
</step>

The third rule links cloudiness back to sunshine. Once we have cloudiness, this rule will let us infer sunshine, which is needed for the first rule.

<logic>sunny(sunrise) :- cloudy(sunrise)</logic>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: either the sunrise is not rainy, or it is storm‑resistant. If we can show the sunrise is rainy, the first option becomes false, forcing the second option to hold. 
<step n="4" type="premise">

<logic>~rainy(sunrise) v storm_resistant(sunrise)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that the sunrise is calm gives us a starting point for the chain of implications. It directly activates the calm‑to‑cloudy rule. 
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The sunrise is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(sunrise)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that the sunrise is calm with the rule that calm implies cloudy, we can conclude the sunrise is cloudy. This is a straightforward application of the conditional, moving us one step forward in the chain. 
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From the fact that the sunrise is calm and the rule that calm implies cloudy, we infer that the sunrise is cloudy.</text>
<logic>cloudy(sunrise)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established cloudiness, we use the cloudy‑to‑sunny rule to infer that the sunrise is sunny. This step is essential because sunshine is the antecedent needed for the sunny‑to‑rainy rule. 
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">

</step>

We now possess the fact that the sunrise is rainy, which directly contradicts the first part of the disjunction "not rainy". Since "not rainy" is false, the only way the disjunction can remain true is if the sunrise is storm‑resistant. Hence we deduce storm‑resistance. 
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>With the sunrise being rainy and the disjunction that either not rainy or storm‑resistant, we apply disjunctive reasoning to obtain that the sunrise is storm‑resistant.</text>
<logic>storm_resistant(sunrise)</logic>

Therefore Sunrise is storm resistant, so the conclusion is True.

context: The bee kit is bold. If a bee kit is bold, then it is steady. Any bee kit that is steady is also busy. Either the bee kit is not busy, or it is pollination driven.
question: Is the bee kit pollination driven?
completion:
<think>
We are given several facts and conditional statements about a particular bee kit. Our goal is to decide whether this bee kit can be described as pollination driven.

The first premise tells us that steadiness guarantees busyness for any bee kit. This will allow us to move from a steady state to a busy state later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any bee kit that is steady is also busy.</text>
<logic>busy(bee_kit) :- steady(bee_kit)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links boldness to steadiness, showing that any bold bee kit must also be steady. This connection is crucial for deriving steadiness from the known bold fact.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a bee kit is bold, then it is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(bee_kit) :- bold(bee_kit)</logic>
</step>

The third premise presents a disjunction: either the bee kit is not busy, or it is pollination driven. This will become useful once we establish the busy status of the kit.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the bee kit is not busy, or it is pollination driven.</text>
<logic>~busy(bee_kit) v pollination_driven(bee_kit)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that the bee kit is bold. This fact serves as the starting point for applying the conditional rules that follow.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The bee kit is bold.</text>
<logic>bold(bee_kit)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the bee kit is bold together with the rule that bold implies steady, we can infer that the bee kit is steady. This is a direct application of modus ponens on premise 2.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Because the bee kit is bold and bold implies steady, we conclude the bee kit is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(bee_kit)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the bee kit is steady, we apply the rule that steadiness leads to busyness. By modus ponens on premise 1, we conclude the bee kit is busy.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since the bee kit is steady and steadiness leads to busyness, the bee kit is busy.</text>
<logic>busy(bee_kit)</logic>
</step>

We have established that the bee kit is busy, which makes the left side of the disjunction in premise 3 false. Since the disjunction must be true, the only remaining possibility is that the bee kit is pollination driven. This follows the pattern of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>We have that the bee kit is busy, and we know either it is not busy or it is pollination driven; the first alternative is false, so we infer it is pollination driven.</text>
<logic>pollination_driven(bee_kit)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Bee_kit is pollination driven, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

<logic>code_compliance(city_planner_jack) :- community_respect(city_planner_jack)</logic>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: either Jack is not code‑compliant, or the residents are satisfied. When we later show Jack is code‑compliant, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that residents are satisfied.

context: In the study of language learners, Ash is described as a person with high aims. Whenever Ash is not fluent, that guarantees Ash is ready to speak. Conversely, if Ash is fluent, then Ash does not have high aims. These two conditional statements, together with the fact that Ash indeed has high aims, constitute all the information we have about Ash's linguistic profile.
question: Based on the given information, is Ash ready to speak?
completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Ash is ready to speak according to the supplied linguistic facts and rules. The goal is to chain the available information so that we can derive the property "ready_to_speak" for Ash.

The first conditional will be useful later because it connects the absence of fluency directly to the desired outcome of readiness. Recognizing this link early helps us see where to apply it once we know Ash's fluency status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The first rule tells us that if Ash is not fluent, then Ash is ready to speak.</text>
<logic>ready_to_speak(ash) :- ~fluent(ash)</logic>
</step>

The second conditional provides a way to reason about Ash's fluency by referencing the opposite of high aims. It will allow us to turn the fact that Ash does have high aims into information about fluency.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The second rule says that if Ash is fluent, then Ash does not have high aims.</text>
<logic>~high_aims(ash) :- fluent(ash)</logic>
</step>

The explicit statement that Ash has high aims gives us a concrete fact to work with. This fact will interact with the second rule to produce a new conclusion about fluency.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>We are told directly that Ash has high aims.</text>
<logic>high_aims(ash)</logic>
</step>

Because the second rule says that fluency would force Ash not to have high aims, and we already know Ash does have high aims, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to reject the antecedent of that rule. Hence we deduce that Ash is not fluent. This is a classic use of modus tollens: the consequent is false, so the antecedent must also be false.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Using the fact that Ash has high aims and the rule that fluency would imply the opposite, we infer that Ash cannot be fluent; therefore Ash is not fluent.</text>
<logic>~fluent(ash)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Ash is not fluent, we can apply the first rule, which states that non‑fluency guarantees readiness to speak. By modus ponens, the truth of the antecedent (~fluent) directly yields the truth of the consequent (ready_to_speak).
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Given that Ash is not fluent and the rule linking non‑fluency to readiness, we conclude that Ash is ready to speak.</text>
<logic>ready_to_speak(ash)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Ash is ready to speak, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

We start with the explicit fact that Walter is inventive. The rule linking inventiveness to strategic behavior tells us that any inventive individual is also strategic. By applying this rule directly to Walter, we can safely conclude that Walter is strategic. This is a straightforward use of modus ponens: the antecedent holds, so the consequent follows.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">

<logic>strategic(walter)</logic>

Since Walter is already known to be strategic, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to reject the possibility that he is scholarly. Thus, we infer that Walter is not scholarly by modus tollens: the consequent is false, so the antecedent must be false.

Having established that Walter is organized, we can invoke the rule that organized individuals are ambitious. The antecedent of this rule is now true for Walter, so we can directly assert that Walter is ambitious. Again, this follows the modus ponens pattern: the condition is met, therefore the conclusion follows.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">

<logic>ambitious(walter)</logic>
</step>

We now know Walter is ambitious, and we also have a disjunction stating that either Walter is not ambitious or he is skillful. Since the first part of the disjunction is contradicted by our knowledge that Walter is indeed ambitious, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the second part to be true. <step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">

We now have the fact that summit_seeker is gear ready, while the rule states that any cooperative climber is not gear ready. If summit_seeker were cooperative, they would have to be not gear ready, which contradicts the gear ready fact we just derived. By modus tollens, the only consistent conclusion is that summit_seeker is not cooperative.

<text>The evidence sample is reliable.</text>

</step>

</step>

context: In the studied river, geologists have observed that it possesses a distinct layered structure. Whenever the river exhibits magmatic activity, it lacks this layered structure. If the river has a stable flow, then it contains valuable ore. The river either shows magmatic activity or has a stable flow.
question: Based on the information, can we conclude that the river does not contain valuable ore?
completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the river lacks valuable ore. To answer this, we need to see if the river can be shown to contain valuable ore using the provided geological observations and conditional relationships.

The first rule links stable flow to valuable ore. Keeping this in mind, if we can establish that the river has stable flow, we will immediately obtain the presence of valuable ore.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the river has stable flow, then it contains valuable ore.</text>
<logic>valuable_ore(river) :- stable_flow(river)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that magmatic activity prevents the river from having a layered structure. This relationship will be useful when we combine it with the fact that the river does possess a layered structure.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the river shows magmatic activity, then it does not have a layered structure.</text>
<logic>~layered_structure(river) :- magmatic_activity(river)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that the river either shows magmatic activity or enjoys stable flow. This alternative will let us eliminate one option and conclude the other once we have evidence against one of them.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The river either shows magmatic activity or has stable flow.</text>
<logic>magmatic_activity(river) v stable_flow(river)</logic>
</step>

The factual observation that the river has a layered structure is a concrete piece of data we can use together with the conditional about magmatic activity.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The river has a layered structure.</text>
<logic>layered_structure(river)</logic>
</step>

Because the river is known to have a layered structure, the conditional that magmatic activity would remove that structure cannot hold. Therefore, the river cannot be exhibiting magmatic activity. This is a classic application of modus tollens, turning a "if‑then" into a negated antecedent.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since the river has a layered structure, it cannot be the case that it shows magmatic activity; therefore the river does not show magmatic activity.</text>
<logic>~magmatic_activity(river)</logic>
</step>

Having ruled out magmatic activity, the earlier disjunction leaves only the other possibility: stable flow. By disjunctive syllogism, if one of the two alternatives is false, the remaining one must be true, so we infer that the river has stable flow.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Given that the river does not show magmatic activity and that it either shows magmatic activity or has stable flow, we infer that the river must have stable flow.</text>
<logic>stable_flow(river)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established stable flow, we can invoke the first rule again. Stable flow guarantees the presence of valuable ore, so we conclude that the river indeed contains valuable ore.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Because the river has stable flow, the rule tells us that it contains valuable ore.</text>
<logic>valuable_ore(river)</logic>
</step>

We derived River is valuable ore, so the conclusion River is not valuable ore is False.
</think>
Answer: False

question: Is Lia productive?
completion:
<think>

<logic>curious(lia)</logic>

context: In the ancient tomb called burial_robin, archaeologists have recorded that the site is undisturbed. They also work with two established principles: any burial that is not modest is well-documented, and any burial that is modest is not undisturbed. These statements together capture all of the facts and rules needed for the reasoning.
question: Based on the information, is burial_robin well-documented?
completion:
<think>
We are given information about the tomb burial_robin and need to decide whether it is well-documented. The goal is to use the provided facts and conditional rules to derive the status of well_documented(burial_robin).

This premise links the absence of modesty to being well-documented, which will be crucial once we know whether the burial is modest or not. It sets up the forward direction of the argument we will eventually use.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a burial is not modest, then it is well-documented.</text>
<logic>well_documented(burial_robin) :- ~modest(burial_robin)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that modesty would force the burial to lose its undisturbed status. By contrasting this with the known fact that the burial is undisturbed, we can reason about modesty itself.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a burial is modest, then it is not undisturbed.</text>
<logic>~undisturbed(burial_robin) :- modest(burial_robin)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The burial burial_robin is undisturbed.</text>
<logic>undisturbed(burial_robin)</logic>
</step>

We start from the fact that burial_robin is undisturbed. If it were modest, the second rule would require it to be not undisturbed, which contradicts the fact. Therefore, modestity must be false, giving us ~modest(burial_robin). This is a classic use of modus tollens.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Because the burial is undisturbed and a modest burial would have to be not undisturbed, we infer that the burial cannot be modest; therefore it is not modest.</text>
<logic>~modest(burial_robin)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established ~modest(burial_robin), we can apply the first rule, which says that a non‑modest burial is well-documented. By modus ponens, we directly infer well_documented(burial_robin).
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Having established that the burial is not modest, and knowing that a non‑modest burial is well-documented, we conclude that the burial is well-documented.</text>
<logic>well_documented(burial_robin)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Burial_robin is well documented, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

<logic>blooming(tomato) :- fruiting(tomato)</logic>
</step>

The conditional that a well_watered tomato cannot be blooming gives us a way to refute well_watered by showing blooming. It sets up a contrapositive argument for the final step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the tomato plant is well_watered, then it is not blooming.</text>
<logic>~blooming(tomato) :- well_watered(tomato)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">

</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The tomato plant is deep_rooted.</text>
<logic>deep_rooted(tomato)</logic>
</step>

We know the tomato is deep_rooted, and the disjunction says either it is not deep_rooted or it is fruiting. Because the first alternative is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that the tomato is fruiting. Hence we can safely derive fruiting for the tomato.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>From the fact that the tomato is deep_rooted and the disjunction that it is either not deep_rooted or fruiting, we infer that the tomato must be fruiting.</text>

question: Is the solar farm not monitored?
completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the solar farm is not monitored. The goal is to prove the negation of the monitored property for the solar farm using the supplied facts and conditional statements.

The rule that an adaptable entity is certified is directly relevant because we already know the solar farm is adaptable. By applying this rule we can obtain a positive statement about the solar farm that will later help us reason about its monitored status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Because the solar farm is adaptable, it follows that it is certified.</text>
<logic>certified(solar_farm) :- adaptable(solar_farm)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links monitoring to a lack of certification: if the solar farm were monitored, it would fail to be certified. This contrapositive relationship will allow us to use the certified fact to deny the monitored condition.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the solar farm were monitored, then it could not be certified.</text>
<logic>~certified(solar_farm) :- monitored(solar_farm)</logic>
</step>

<text>The wind turbine is data guided.</text>
<logic>data_guided(wind_turbine)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The solar farm is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(solar_farm)</logic>
</step>

We have the fact that the solar farm is adaptable (Premise 4) and the rule that adaptability implies certification (Premise 1). Using modus ponens, we combine these to conclude that the solar farm is certified. This derived fact is essential for the next step.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the solar farm being adaptable we infer that the solar farm is certified.</text>
<logic>certified(solar_farm)</logic>
</step>

Now we possess the certified status of the solar farm (Step 5) and the conditional that monitoring would prevent certification (Premise 2). Applying modus tollens, the existence of certification forces the antecedent—being monitored—to be false, so we infer the solar farm is not monitored.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since the solar farm is certified and a monitored solar farm would not be certified, we conclude the solar farm is not monitored.</text>
<logic>~monitored(solar_farm)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Solar_farm is not monitored, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

context: Rex is known to be adaptable. Whenever Rex is calm, he becomes productive, so any calm Rex is also productive. Conversely, if Rex is scholarly then he is not productive; being scholarly guarantees the lack of productivity. Regarding possibilities, either Rex is not adaptable or he is well prepared, and either Rex is not well prepared or he is calm. These statements together capture all the facts, rules, and disjunctions that will be used in the proof.
question: Is Rex scholarly?
completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Rex possesses the property of being scholarly. To answer this, we will follow the chain of premises and derived facts provided, checking how each influences the next.

The first premise gives us a direct conditional: calm leads to productivity. This will become useful later when we manage to establish that Rex is calm, allowing us to infer productivity.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Rex is calm then Rex is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(rex) :- calm(rex)</logic>
</step>

The second premise provides the converse conditional: being scholarly forces the negation of productivity. This creates a tension that will let us test the scholarly claim once we know Rex's productivity status.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Rex is scholarly then Rex is not productive.</text>
<logic>~productive(rex) :- scholarly(rex)</logic>
</step>

The third premise is a disjunction offering two alternatives about adaptability and preparation. It sets up a logical choice that, together with the fact about adaptability, will let us eliminate one option.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Rex is not adaptable or Rex is well prepared.</text>
<logic>~adaptable(rex) v well_prepared(rex)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise is another disjunction linking well‑preparedness and calmness. It will allow us to move from a conclusion about being well prepared to a conclusion about being calm.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Rex is not well prepared or Rex is calm.</text>
<logic>~well_prepared(rex) v calm(rex)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Rex is adaptable. This concrete fact will interact with the first disjunction to force the other disjunct, namely that Rex is well prepared.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given as a fact that Rex is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(rex)</logic>
</step>

Because Rex is adaptable, the disjunction "not adaptable or well prepared" cannot be satisfied by the first part; therefore the second part must hold, giving us that Rex is well prepared. This is a standard application of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>From the fact that Rex is adaptable and the disjunction that either Rex is not adaptable or he is well prepared, we infer that Rex must be well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(rex)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Rex well prepared, we look at the second disjunction "not well prepared or calm". Since the first option is false (Rex is well prepared), the second must be true, so Rex is calm. Again, disjunctive syllogism lets us eliminate the false alternative.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Using the fact that Rex is well prepared together with the disjunction that either Rex is not well prepared or he is calm, we derive that Rex is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(rex)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Rex is calm, we can invoke the first rule which says calm implies productive. By modus ponens, this yields that Rex is productive. This links the calm state directly to productivity.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Rex is calm, the rule that calm implies productive lets us conclude that Rex is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(rex)</logic>
</step>

With Rex productive in hand, we turn to the second rule: scholarly leads to not productive. If Rex were scholarly, he would have to be not productive, contradicting the fact that he is productive. Thus, by modus tollens, we conclude Rex cannot be scholarly.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Given that Rex is productive, the rule that scholarly implies not productive lets us infer that Rex cannot be scholarly.</text>
<logic>~scholarly(rex)</logic>
</step>

We derived Rex is not scholarly, so the conclusion Rex is scholarly is False.
</think>
Answer: False

context: In the wildlife conservation team, Helen is known to be capable. The team notes that whenever Helen is not attentive, she becomes astute. question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Helen is not motivated?
completion:

<step n="2" type="premise">

<logic>~astute(helen) :- motivated(helen)</logic>
</step>

The third rule links attentiveness to the absence of dedication. Knowing whether Helen is dedicated will let us work backwards to learn about her attentiveness, which is needed for the first rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is attentive, then she is not dedicated.</text>
<logic>~dedicated(helen) :- attentive(helen)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction offers two alternatives: Helen is either not capable or she is meticulous. Because we already know she is capable, the only way for the statement to hold is that she must be meticulous.

<logic>~capable(helen) v meticulous(helen)</logic>

The second disjunction says Helen is either not meticulous or dedicated. Once we have shown she is meticulous, the only remaining way for the disjunction to be true is that she must be dedicated.

</step>

Having both the fact capable(helen) and the disjunction ~capable(helen) ∨ meticulous(helen), we apply disjunctive syllogism: the left side of the disjunction is false, so the right side must hold. Therefore, Helen is meticulous.

Now that we know meticulous(helen) and have the disjunction ~meticulous(helen) ∨ dedicated(helen), we again use disjunctive syllogism. The left option is false, forcing the right option to be true, so Helen is dedicated.

<logic>dedicated(helen)</logic>

From dedicated(helen) and the rule ~dedicated(helen) :- attentive(helen) (which reads attentive → not dedicated), we use the contrapositive: if someone is dedicated, they cannot be attentive. Thus we conclude that Helen is not attentive.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since Helen is dedicated and the rule linking attentiveness to non‑dedication, we conclude that Helen is not attentive.</text>
<logic>~attentive(helen)</logic>
</step>

With the established fact ~attentive(helen) and the rule astute(helen) :- ~attentive(helen), modus ponens gives us astute(helen). This new property will be crucial for the final step.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Given that Helen is not attentive, the first rule lets us deduce that Helen is astute.</text>
<logic>astute(helen)</logic>
</step>

We have astute(helen) and the rule ~astute(helen) :- motivated(helen) (motivated → not astute). Taking the contrapositive, if someone is astute, they cannot be motivated. Hence we derive ~motivated(helen).

Therefore Helen is not motivated, so the conclusion is True.
